rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM BETTENCOURT et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B/P)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, May 11, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 3. Defendant Hennessy Industries, Inc.¿s motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted with respect to all causes of action alleged against Hennessy. The second, third and sixth causes of action do not specify any product for which defendant is responsible. (Bockrath v. Aldrich Chemical Co., Inc. (1999) 21 Cal.4th 71, 80). The sixteenth and seventeenth causes of action allege that the brake shoe grinding machines ¿while being used in a manner that was reasonably foreseeable, failed to protect decedent from exposure to asbestos fibers released from asbestos-containing brake lining caused by use of said brake shoe grinding machines and related products and equipment, resulting in severe and permanent injury to decedent.¿ (Complaint ¶¶ 164, 169). The machines are alleged to be ¿defective and unsafe for their intended purpose in that said products caused, and failed to prevent, the inhalation and ingestion of asbestos fibers by decedent. (Complaint ¶¶ 165, 170). A product manufacturer has no duty to persons whose injury results from defects in the products of others. (Garman v. Magic Chef, Inc. (1981) 117 Cal.App.3d 634; Walton v. William Powell Co. 2010 WL 1612209; Taylor v. Elliott Turbomachinery Co., Inc. 171 (2009) Cal.App.4th 564). The complaint does not allege that defendant sold an asbestos-containing product or that it had any control over the design and manufacture of the asbestos-containing brake linings used in conjunction with its machine. Tellez-Cordova v. Campbell-Hausfeld/Scott Fetzger Co. (2004) 129 Cal.App.4th 577 is distinguishable because the power tools at issue in that case had no function until they were used with toxic-containing disks. (Id. at 584 (¿[U]se with the specified wheels, discs, and grinders was the inevitable use.¿). (to be Contined in the next entry.)

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM BETTENCOURT et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B/P)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Hennessy Industries, Inc.¿S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Continued From The Previous Entry)
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Tuesday, May 11, 2010 in Department 220 at 9:30 a.m., Line 3. Defendant Hennessy Industries, Inc.¿S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (Continued From The Previous Entry). Here, there is no allegation that defendant¿s machines could only function in conjunction with asbestos-containing brake linings, or that the machines and linings formed a single defective system over which the defendant exercised significant control. (Walton, WL 1612209 at *7 (¿Tellez-Cordova stands for the proposition that the component parts doctrine is inapplicable when a manufacturer's product is uniquely designed to complete a system that is hazardous in its intended use¿.Unlike Tellez-Cordova, in which the tools and discs formed a single system over which the tool manufacturers had significant control, the combination of Powell's valves with the packing, gaskets, and insulation formed no such system.¿). The machines are more analogous to the stove in Garman and the acid in Blackwell v. Phelps Dodge Corp. (1984) 157 Cal.App.3d 372. As in these cases, the plaintiff¿s injury would not have occurred but for the defendant¿s product, but it was a defect in the product of another which proximately caused the injury. The only alleged defect in the defendant¿s machines is that they either caused another defective product to injure the decedent Under Garman and Blackwell, the defendant has no duty to plaintiff under these facts. Plaintiffs¿ request for leave to amend with respect to its third cause of action is denied on the ground that plaintiffs have not shown that they could amend their complaint to allege liability against Hennessy. =(220/HEK)
